title: Big-O Notation

project_usage: Evaluating API endpoint scalability: will this handler survive 10x traffic growth?
Choosing between data structures (hash map O(1) lookup vs. sorted array O(log n) lookup) in system design

glossary.definition: The maximum limit on how much time or memory an algorithm can use.
A fixed multiplier that affects actual speed but not the overall scaling category.
The memory structure used by a computer to keep track of a function calling itself.

overview.what: Big-O notation describes the upper bound on an algorithm's growth rate as the input size approaches infinity. It strips away constants and lower-order terms, capturing only the dominant factor that determines how the algorithm scales. O(f(n)) means there exist constants c and n0 such that T(n) <= c * f(n) for all n >= n0.
overview.why: Exact operation counts like 3n^2 + 7n + 4 are precise but overwhelming to compare. Big-O compresses them to their essential scaling behavior — O(n^2) — so you can instantly decide whether an algorithm is feasible for a given input size without drowning in arithmetic.
where_used: Every technical interview: candidates must state and justify the Big-O of their solution before it is considered complete
System design discussions: estimating whether a proposed architecture handles expected load (e.g., O(n) indexing vs. O(log n) B-tree lookups for millions of records)

why_learning_this: It is the universal language for discussing algorithm efficiency — no technical interview or design review proceeds without it.
It teaches you to ignore noise (constants, hardware) and focus on the structural scaling property that actually determines real-world performance at scale.
Misusing Big-O (e.g., claiming O(n) when the algorithm is O(n^2)) is one of the fastest ways to lose credibility in a technical discussion.

common_mistakes.title: Thinking Big-O measures exact speed
common_mistakes.explanation: Big-O measures the growth RATE, not actual time. An O(n) algorithm with a huge constant factor can be slower than an O(n^2) algorithm for small inputs. Big-O tells you what happens as n grows large.

common_mistakes.title: Dropping terms from different variables
common_mistakes.explanation: If an algorithm iterates over n items and then over m items, the complexity is O(n + m), not O(n). You can only drop terms when they share the same variable and one dominates the other.

common_mistakes.title: Confusing Big-O (upper bound) with exact complexity
common_mistakes.explanation: Saying an O(n) algorithm is O(n^2) is technically true — O(n^2) is a valid upper bound — but it is uselessly loose. In interviews, always give the tightest upper bound that accurately describes the algorithm.

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting that Big-O applies to space too
common_mistakes.explanation: Big-O is not just for time. Storing n elements in a hash map is O(n) space; a recursive call stack that goes n levels deep uses O(n) space. Always analyze both dimensions.

recall_questions.q: State the formal definition of Big-O in one sentence.
recall_questions.answer: T(n) is O(f(n)) if there exist positive constants c and n0 such that T(n) <= c * f(n) for all n >= n0.

recall_questions.q: Why does Big-O drop constants and lower-order terms?
recall_questions.answer: Because as n approaches infinity, the highest-order term dominates and constants become irrelevant to the growth rate. Dropping them isolates the structural scaling behavior.

recall_questions.q: An algorithm does 3n^2 + 100n + 500 operations. What is its Big-O, and which terms were dropped and why?
recall_questions.answer: O(n^2). The 100n and 500 are lower-order terms that grow slower than n^2, and the coefficient 3 is a constant multiplier — all are irrelevant to asymptotic growth.

understanding_checks.question: A developer says: 'My algorithm runs in 0.5 seconds so it must be O(1).' Why is this reasoning flawed?
understanding_checks.answer: Big-O is not a measure of absolute time — it describes how time GROWS with input size. A 0.5-second run on one input tells you nothing about the growth rate. The algorithm might be O(n^3) on a small input that happened to be fast.
understanding_checks.why: Confusing measured time with asymptotic growth rate is one of the most dangerous misconceptions because it makes engineers believe their solution scales when it does not.

understanding_checks.question: You have two sorting algorithms: Algorithm X has T(n) = 1000n*log(n) and Algorithm Y has T(n) = 2n^2. For n = 10, Y is faster. For n = 1,000,000, which is faster and why does Big-O predict this?
understanding_checks.answer: For n = 1,000,000, Algorithm X (O(n log n)) is vastly faster. X does about 20 billion operations vs. Y's 2 trillion. Big-O predicted this because n*log(n) grows strictly slower than n^2 — the crossover is inevitable and the gap widens forever.
understanding_checks.why: This demonstrates exactly why Big-O drops constants: 1000 is a huge coefficient, but it cannot prevent n*log(n) from eventually beating n^2. The growth rate always wins at scale.

aha_moment.prediction: The constant 999999 dominates for small n, so all three terms matter.
aha_moment.common_guess: The lower-order terms always matter because they add to the total.
aha_moment.why: At n = 10, the constant term is 99% of the work and the n^2 term is negligible. But at n = 1,000,000, the n^2 term is 99.99998% of the work and everything else vanishes. THIS is why Big-O keeps only the highest-order term — it predicts the reality at scale, where performance actually matters.

practice_tasks.title: Simplify operation counts to Big-O
practice_tasks.prompt: For each of the following T(n) expressions, identify the dominant term and write the Big-O: (a) 7n + 3, (b) 4n^2 + 99n + 12, (c) 2^n + n^3, (d) 500, (e) n*log(n) + n. Justify each by explaining which terms were dropped and why.
practice_tasks.solution: (a) O(n) — drop constant 3 and coefficient 7. (b) O(n^2) — n^2 dominates n and constants. (c) O(2^n) — exponential dwarfs any polynomial. (d) O(1) — constant work regardless of input. (e) O(n log n) — n*log(n) grows faster than n.
